title: Middle East Escalation and Energy Price Shock
content: The most significant market driver in March 2026 was the dramatic military escalation involving the US, Israel, and Iran, which severely disrupted global energy supplies. A blockade of the Strait of Hormuz saw daily tanker traffic plunge from over 50 to fewer than 15, catapulting oil prices above the psychological $100/barrel threshold. This supply-side shock reversed years of steady disinflationary trends, with both the Fed and BoE warning that energy costs could add a full percentage point to headline CPI. Markets transitioned from pricing in aggressive rate cuts to a 'higher-for-longer' regime as insurance costs for maritime trade surged. The shock hit global growth projections, particularly in Europe, where the ECB revised its 2026 growth forecast down to 0.9%, reflecting the immediate impact of energy volatility on industrial output and consumer confidence.

title: Equities Enter Correction as AI Narrative Softens
content: US equities faced their worst performance of the year, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq both entering formal correction territory—defined as a 10% drop from recent peaks. The downturn was characterised by a sharp rotation out of AI-related software and growth stocks as investors questioned the immediate return on massive AI capital expenditures. Even stellar earnings from sector leaders like Nvidia were met with selling pressure, suggesting a 'peak hype' phase had concluded. The volatility was exacerbated by 'Trumpflation' fears and the proposed 15% global import surcharge, which threatened multinational margins. By late March, the Dow Jones had recorded five consecutive losing weeks, its longest such streak in four years, as the broader market shifted focus from speculative growth toward defensive 'value' sectors and hard assets.
